verses.text: Mas muito cedo na manhã de domingo: A igreja primitiva adorava no primeiro dia da semana (domingo) em vez do sétimo (o sábado) porque Jesus ressuscitou em uma manhã de domingo (cp. **At 20.7** ; **Ap 1.10** ).
A pedra era provavelmente uma grande pedra redonda que foi rolada por um rastro para cobrir a entrada do túmulo.

24:3 And found not the body of the Lord - His holy soul was in Paradise, Luke 23:43; and the evangelist mentions the body particularly, to show that this only was subject to death. It is, I think, evident enough, from these and other words of Luke, that the doctrine of the materiality of the soul, made no part of his creed.

dois homens de repente apareceram: Anjos frequentemente aparecem como jovens nas Escrituras ( **Gn 18.2** ; **19.1** , **10** ; **Js 5.13** ; **Jz 13.6-11** ; **Hb 13.2** ). Vestes brancas deslumbrantes simbolizam pureza e santidade ( **Dn 10.5-6** ; **At 1.10** ; **Ap 4.4** ; **19.14** ).

Jesus havia dito aos seus seguidores muitas vezes que ele ressuscitaria dos mortos (veja **9.22** , **44** ; **17.25** ; **18.31-34** ). A ressurreição de Jesus é um princípio fundamental da fé cristã (veja **1Co 15.3-7** , **12-57** ).

Jesus havia dito aos seus seguidores muitas vezes que ele ressuscitaria dos mortos (veja **9.22** , **44** ; **17.25** ; **18.31-34** ). A ressurreição de Jesus é um princípio fundamental da fé cristã (veja **1Co 15.3-7** , **12-57** ).
Jesus ressuscitou no terceiro dia, contando sexta-feira como o primeiro dia e domingo como o terceiro.

24:8 They remembered his words - Even the simple recollection of the words of Christ becomes often a source of comfort and support to those who are distressed or tempted: for his words are the words of eternal life.
Seus onze discípulos eram os Doze menos Judas.
Maria Madalena e Joana são mencionadas em **8.2-3** . Maria, a mãe de Tiago, não é a mãe de Tiago, filho de Zebedeu (veja **Mt 27.56** ), mas pode ser a mãe de Tiago, filho de Alfeu ( **Lc 6.15** ).

24:11 And behold two of them went that same day,.... Two of the disciples, as the Persic version reads; not of the eleven apostles, for it is certain that one of them was not an apostle; but two of the seventy disciples, or of the society of the hundred and twenty that were together: one of these was Cleophas or Alphaeus, as appears from Luke 24:18 the other is, by some, thought to be Luke the Evangelist, as Theophylact on the place observes, who, out of modesty, mentions not his name;
Pedro... correu para o túmulo para olhar: João também acompanhou Pedro ( **Jo 20.2-9** ).

dois seguidores de Jesus: Jesus tinha muitos discípulos além dos Doze (veja **10.1** , **17** ). Nada mais se sabe sobre Cleopas ( **24.18** ) ou o outro discípulo (que pode ter sido sua esposa). Eles provavelmente estavam retornando da celebração da Páscoa em Jerusalém.

Este relato é a contribuição única de Lucas para as narrativas da ressurreição. Jesus corrigiu o mal-entendido desses discípulos mostrando pelas Escrituras que era necessário que o Messias sofresse. Ele então abriu os olhos deles para a verdade de sua ressurreição.\n\n

Este relato é a contribuição única de Lucas para as narrativas da ressurreição. Jesus corrigiu o mal-entendido desses discípulos mostrando pelas Escrituras que era necessário que o Messias sofresse. Ele então abriu os olhos deles para a verdade de sua ressurreição.\n\n

Deus os impediu de reconhecê-lo: O grego usa uma construção passiva ( eles foram impedidos de reconhecê-lo ) conhecida como um “passivo divino”, com Deus como o sujeito implícito. Deus intencionalmente cegou seus olhos até o momento da revelação, quando Jesus partiu o pão com eles ( **24.30-31** ; cp. **2Rs 6.17** ).

24:17 And he said unto them, what things?.... Still appearing as if he was ignorant, and in order to, draw out of them a particular relation: and they said unto him; both of them, or rather Cleophas, for himself and his companion: concerning Jesus of Nazareth; that is, what had happened to him, who was commonly known by this name, and was called so by way of contempt: but which was a prophet; not only a foreteller of things to come, as he foretold his sufferings, death, and resurrection, the troubles that should befall his disciples, the destruction of Jerusalem, and the end of the world;

a única pessoa em Jerusalém que não ouviu falar sobre todas as coisas: Os romanos realizavam crucificações em cruzamentos públicos importantes para dar exemplo das vítimas e alertar outros contra a revolta. Era impensável para esses discípulos que um peregrino da Páscoa não tivesse ouvido falar da crucificação de Jesus.
Jesus é frequentemente retratado como um profeta no Evangelho de Lucas (veja **7.16** ; cp. a descrição de Moisés em **At 7.22** ).

24:20 Yea, and certain women also of our company,.... Who came with them from Galilee, who were of the same religion, professed the same faith, and belonged to the same society and community; who they were, and their names, see Luke 24:10. made us astonished; surprised us, with an account they brought, so that we could not, nor can we now tell, what to think or say of it; it is such an one, we know not how to believe, nor to disprove;

Tínhamos esperança de que ele fosse o Messias: Literalmente tínhamos esperança de que ele fosse o que libertaria Israel. Esta é uma referência ao Messias — a crucificação havia destruído suas esperanças.
um relato incrível: Veja **24.10-11** .

24:23 Then he said unto them,.... That is, Jesus said unto them, as the Syriac and Persic versions read: O fools; not in a natural sense, as if they were destitute of the common understanding of men; nor in a moral sense, as wicked men, and as they themselves had been in their unregenerate estate; nor in a way of anger and contempt, and with a design to provoke; wherefore Christ did not act contrary to his own rule, in Matt 5:22 but because they were so void of understanding in the Scriptures, and were so very ignorant of them, and were so blind as to the knowledge of them;

24:24 Ought not Christ to have suffered these things,.... Mentioned in Luke 24:20 as to be delivered by the chief priests, to be condemned to death, and to be crucified: Christ suffered many things in his personal character, being traduced as a sinful and wicked man, and a friend and encourager of sinners; as a man of immoral principles and practices; as an idolater, a blasphemer, an impostor, a seditious person; as one that had had familiarity with the devil, and did his miracles by his assistance, with a load of other reproaches;

os profetas escreveram... que o Messias teria que sofrer: A crucificação não negou a identidade de Jesus como o Messias, mas a confirmou, porque a morte do Messias foi predita nas Escrituras. Jesus repreendeu os dois por não reconhecerem nas Escrituras que o Messias tinha que sofrer (veja **Is 50.4-9** ; **52.13–53.12** ; **Sl 2** , **16** , **22** , **118** ; cp. **Lc 18.32** ; **20.17** ; **23.37** ; **At 2.25-28** ; **4.25-26** ).

os profetas escreveram... que o Messias teria que sofrer: A crucificação não negou a identidade de Jesus como o Messias, mas a confirmou, porque a morte do Messias foi predita nas Escrituras. Jesus repreendeu os dois por não reconhecerem nas Escrituras que o Messias tinha que sofrer (veja **Is 50.4-9** ; **52.13–53.12** ; **Sl 2** , **16** , **22** , **118** ; cp. **Lc 18.32** ; **20.17** ; **23.37** ; **At 2.25-28** ; **4.25-26** ).
Todas as Escrituras , significando todo o Antigo Testamento, apontam para a vinda de Jesus, o Messias, e a salvação que ele traria.

24:28 He made as though he would have gone farther - That is, he was going on, as though he intended to go farther; and so he doubtless would had they not earnestly pressed him to lodge with them. His preaching had made a deep impression upon their hearts, Luke 24:32, and now they feel it their greatest privilege to entertain the preacher. This is a constant effect of the doctrine of Christ: wherever it is felt, the Author of it, the ever-blessed Jesus, is earnestly entreated to dwell in the heart;
eles imploraram a ele: “Fique a noite conosco”: A hospitalidade do Oriente Médio exigia que oferecessem comida e hospedagem a este estranho ( **Gn 18.3** ; **19.2** ; **Jz 19.5-9** ).

Enquanto se sentavam para comer: A refeição é um símbolo do banquete messiânico e da salvação que Deus está realizando ( **Is 25.6-8** ; **Lc 14.1-24** ). • Então ele partiu e deu a eles: Esta ação lembra a alimentação dos 5.000 ( **9.16** ) e a instituição da Ceia do Senhor ( **22.19** ).

seus olhos foram abertos: Este é outro exemplo do “passivo divino” (veja nota de estudo em 24.16 ); Deus abriu seus olhos ao partir do pão. • naquele momento ele desapareceu! Em seu corpo ressuscitado e transformado, Jesus tinha habilidades não percebidas antes da ressurreição (veja **24.36** ; **1Co 15.20-23** , **35-57** ).

24:32 They now tell each to the other how their hearts burned—were fired—within them at His talk and His expositions of Scripture. "Ah! this accounts for it: We could not understand the glow of self-evidencing light, love, glory that ravished our hearts; but now we do." They cannot rest—how could they?—they must go straight back and tell the news. They find the eleven, but ere they have time to tell their tale, their ears are saluted with the thrilling news, "The Lord is risen indeed, and hath appeared to Simon." Most touching and precious intelligence this.

24:33 And they told what things were done in the way,.... That is, when the eleven had done speaking, and had finished their report, which they did with great joy and pleasure; then "these two disciples", as the Persic version expresses it, to confirm them the more in the truth of Christ's resurrection, gave them a particular account, how, as they were travelling, Jesus joined himself to them, and entered into a conversation with them, and opened the Scriptures in a sweet and powerful manner to them;
Pedro: Esta aparição a Simão Pedro não é narrada nos Evangelhos, mas Paulo a confirma independentemente em **1Co 15.5** .

24:35 And they - The two disciples who were just come from Emmaus, related what had happened to them on the way, going to Emmaus, and how he had been known unto them in the breaking of bread, while supping together at the above village. See on Luke 24:31 (note).

24:36 And as they thus spake - While the two disciples who were going to Emmaus were conversing about Christ, he joined himself to their company. Now, while they and the apostles are confirming each other in their belief of his resurrection, Jesus comes in, to remove every doubt, and to give them the fullest evidence of it. And it is ever true that, wherever two or three are gathered together in his name, he is in the midst of them.

24:37 a spirit—the ghost of their dead Lord, but not Himself in the body (Acts 12:15; Matt 14:26). thoughts—rather, "reasonings"; that is, whether He were risen or no, and whether this was His very self.

24:38 And when he had thus spoken,.... And put them into a method of satisfying themselves by their senses: he showed them his hands and his feet; that is, he held them forth to be seen and handled by them, which no doubt they did; and which were the infallible proofs by which he showed himself alive to them after his passion; and by which they knew the truth of his incarnation, or that he assumed a true and real body, and of the resurrection of the same body;

fantasmas não têm corpos, como vocês vêm que eu tenho: Em seu estado ressuscitado, Jesus não era um espírito imaterial, mas tinha um corpo real, imortal e imperecível. Os crentes receberão corpos semelhantes na ressurreição final ( **1Co 15.35-57** ).

24:40 And they gave him a piece of a broiled fish,.... Which was left by them at supper, of which they had been eating; for being fishermen, most of them, this was agreeable food to them: and of an honeycomb; not to eat with the fish, but after it.

24:41 They - believed not for joy - They were so overcome with the joy of his resurrection, that they did not, for some time, properly receive the evidence that was before them - as we phrase it, they thought the news too good to be true.

24:42 Then opened he their understanding,.... He not only opened their hearts, to attend to what he said, and the proofs he gave of his resurrection from the dead in a true body; but he removed the veil from the eyes of their minds, and gave them an understanding of the sacred writings, respecting this matter: that they might understand the Scriptures; concerning his sufferings, death, and resurrection, which they were very ignorant of before, and which were as a sealed book unto them, John 20:9 though they had been from their infancy brought up to the reading of the Scriptures, and had had the advantage of Christ's ministry for some years;

24:43 And he said unto them, thus it is written,.... In the above cited books of the Law, the Prophets, and the Psalms; not what after follows, in so many words, but the matter and substance thereof: and thus it behoved Christ to suffer, and to rise from the dead the third day; it was necessary, both because of the decree and appointment of God, and because of the prophecies and predictions of the Old Testament; see Luke 24:25.

a lei de Moisés e os profetas e nos Salmos: Jesus referiu-se às três seções das Escrituras Hebraicas: a Lei, os Profetas e os Escritos. Todo o Antigo Testamento aponta para a salvação de Deus através de Cristo.

24:45 Then opened he, &c.—a statement of unspeakable value; expressing, on the one hand, Christ's immediate access to the human spirit and absolute power over it, to the adjustment of its vision, and permanent rectification for spiritual discernment (than which it is impossible to conceive a stronger evidence of His proper divinity); and, on the other hand, making it certain that the manner of interpreting the ld Testament which the apostles afterwards employed (see the Acts and Epistles), has the direct sanction of Christ Himself.
que o Messias sofreria e morreria: Veja nota de estudo em 24.25-26 .
O Antigo Testamento foi a proclamação universal do Evangelho para todas as nações: Veja **Is 42.6** ; **49.6** ; **Jl 2.28-32** ; **Am 9.11-12** ; **At 2.17-21** ; **13.47** ; **15.16-18** .
O papel principal dos apóstolos no livro de Atos era ser testemunhas do cumprimento das Escrituras na vida, morte e ressurreição de Jesus (veja **At 1.8** ).

Eu enviarei o Espírito Santo: O Antigo Testamento previu que Deus derramaria seu Espírito sobre todas as pessoas ( **Is 32.15** ; **Jr 31.33** ; **Ez 36.26-27** ; **Jl 2.28-32** ). Esta previsão foi cumprida no dia de Pentecostes ( **At 2.14-21** ).
Betânia: Veja nota de estudo em 19.29 ; veja também **At 1.11-12** ; cp. **Zc 14.4** .
Cp. **2Rs 2.11** . A ascensão de Jesus foi sua exaltação a uma posição de autoridade sobre toda a criação (veja **At 2.32-36** ; **5.31** ; **Ef 1.19-23** ; **Fl 2.9-11** ; **Hb 1.13** ).

Lucas também narra a ascensão de Jesus em **At 1.1-11** . Para Lucas, a ascensão de Jesus confirmou sua vindicação, exaltação e entronização à direita de Deus como Messias e Senhor ( **At 2.24-36** ). De sua posição de glória e autoridade no céu, Jesus guia e dirige sua igreja.

O Templo era um lugar de adoração e também de sacrifício. Os apóstolos continuaram em seu modo judaico de adoração porque seu objetivo não era criar uma nova religião, mas anunciar o cumprimento das promessas das Escrituras de salvação através de Jesus, o Messias.
